quote: and now now my mom is gonna be going to hospice care, to to the hospital for probably about four weeks, and I would like to get everything kinda tidied up and ready for her to come back home for her last days.
stewart_read: The customer discloses that her mother is entering hospice for her final weeks of life and that reducing the electric bill is part of preparing the home for her mother's last days. This is a trust gift of the highest order — the customer is telling the rep exactly why this matters emotionally and urgently. The rep's response at 02:12 is 'Yeah. So I can just set up a phone call appointment for you.' No acknowledgment of the hospice disclosure, no bridge to how solar addresses the bill pain she named, no human moment. The reactive read is that the rep simply didn't register the weight of what was said. The strategic read — that the rep was deliberately moving past it to avoid scope creep — does not hold, because empathy acknowledgment is not scope creep; it is the foundation of the appointment's durability.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep was deliberately keeping the call short to avoid scope creep and moved to scheduling as the correct setter move.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the 00:39–01:15 window and ask her what she heard. The customer named her mother's hospice care, her own disability, and her desire to reduce bills as an act of care for her dying mother. A single sentence — 'I'm so sorry you're going through all of this — let's make sure we get this sorted for you' — before moving to scheduling costs nothing and materially changes the closer's starting position. Walk the rep through the schema's empathy_on_vulnerability_disclosure pattern: acknowledge specifically, then bridge to value.

quote: my husband and my daughter right now. So I because I'm disabled myself.
stewart_read: The customer identifies both her husband and her daughter as people she must consult before committing to a time — and the daughter is explicitly named as someone she needs to coordinate with for logistics. The schema's adult_child_decision_maker protocol is the more precise match here: the daughter is functioning as a co-decision-maker and logistics coordinator, not just a family member. The rep never asks whether the husband or daughter should be on the appointment. The spouse_decision protocol applies to the husband (title co-owner status unknown); the adult_child_decision_maker protocol applies to the daughter. Neither was addressed. The reactive read is that the rep missed both. The strategic read — that the rep knew this was a phone call and assumed the customer could handle it alone — does not hold, because the customer's own framing ('I have to consult them') signals she does not make decisions independently.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep assumed that because the appointment is a phone call, the customer could participate without her husband or daughter present.
coaching_implication: At 01:44, the rep had a clear opening: 'It sounds like your husband and daughter are part of these decisions — would it make sense to have one of them on the call with you?' This is the adult_child_decision_maker protocol applied in real time. The manager should walk the rep through why including a co-decision-maker at the appointment stage protects the sit — a closer who presents to one person when two are needed loses the deal at the kitchen table.

quote: Yeah. So I can just set up a phone call appointment for you. It looks like you're already assigned to a specialist. Let me just look at their schedule here.
stewart_read: This is the moment the rep decides the call is a scheduling transaction. The prior appointment was missed before any qualification occurred — the customer is effectively a new lead from a verify standpoint. The rep skips the entire verify section: no homeowner confirmation, no title/co-owner check, no interest-in-solar depth, no utility company, no bill anchor, no roof age, no prior designs. On a paid lead, the bill is required before the appointment can be set. On any lead type, the closer will walk into this appointment with zero qualification data. This is not a strategic compression of verify beats (as might be justified on a recycled lead with data on file per context.lead_types_and_bill_collection_rules) — there is no evidence any prior data was checked. It is a call_shape failure.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep knew this was a recycled lead with qualification data already on file and correctly skipped re-verification.
coaching_implication: The manager should ask the rep directly: 'What did you know about this customer's qualification before you booked the appointment?' If the answer is 'nothing,' this is the coaching moment. The rep should understand that a prior missed appointment does not carry forward qualification data — the verify section exists precisely to protect the closer's time. Even a compressed verify (bill amount, homeowner confirmation, title check) in 60 seconds would have given the closer something to work with.

quote: And we can get everything tightened, and she can have the last number of days and and be healthy and healthy and not worry well, as as healthy as she can and and not have to worry about all of these things because her her electric bill has just been exorbitant.
stewart_read: The customer volunteers the bill pain unprompted and frames it in the most emotionally loaded terms possible — reducing the electric bill is an act of care for her dying mother. This is the bill anchor handing itself to the rep. The rep never picks it up: no 'what does that bill typically run?' follow-up, no flip into alignment-against-the-utility, no acknowledgment that this is the exact problem solar addresses. The bill is mentioned and dropped. Per the schema, capture alone is not sufficient — the bill must be flipped into emotional alignment. Here, the rep didn't even capture it.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep was prioritizing speed on a reschedule call and deferred bill collection to the closer.
coaching_implication: The customer gave the rep the emotional anchor for the entire pitch in one sentence. The manager should replay this moment and ask: 'What would you have said here?' The correct move is a single question — 'Do you know roughly what that bill runs each month?' — followed by the flip: 'That's exactly what we're going to help with.' The rep doesn't need to sell solar; she needs to confirm the pain and tie it to the appointment. This is the bill_anchoring pattern the schema identifies as universally missed across the floor.

quote: I want her to be able stay in her pretty house. Yeah. So Uh-huh. I'd I'd like to see if I could schedule someone to come and meet me.
stewart_read: The customer is not a passive lead — she is actively motivated, has a specific property in mind (her mother's two-story house in Marble Falls), has a clear emotional reason for wanting solar (reducing bills as an act of care), and is re-engaging after a prior missed appointment. This is a high-intent customer. The rep never surfaces or anchors this motivation. The interest_in_solar verify item exists precisely to capture and deepen this kind of emotional driver — the rep walked past it entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep treated the customer's motivation as self-evident and moved to scheduling to respect her time.
coaching_implication: The manager should highlight this as a missed anchor opportunity, not just a verify checkbox. The customer's motivation — keeping her mother in her home for her final days — is the most powerful appointment-durability signal on the call. A rep who names it back ('So this is really about making sure your mom can stay comfortable in her home — that's exactly what we want to help with') creates a closer who walks in knowing why this appointment matters to the customer. That's the difference between a sit and a no-show.

quote: And then I can send you a text message as well with the details of this appointment, just so you can put that on your schedule and have a better reminder. Okay?
stewart_read: The rep executes the text-reminder beat, which is the one button_up element she completes. However, the remaining button_up requirements are absent: no one-hour block stated, no 'not driving / at computer' anchor, no 'is that fair' verbal handshake. More critically, the text she promises will be generic — there is no tieback to the customer's specific situation (mother's hospice, bill pain, Marble Falls property) because the rep never captured those details in a way that could be reflected back. The schema requires the text to reference something specific the customer said. A generic 'looking forward to chatting' text on this call is a missed durability move.
alternative_hypothesis: Rep kept the button_up light intentionally to avoid overwhelming a customer who had already disclosed significant stress.
coaching_implication: The manager should walk the rep through the full button_up checklist and ask which elements she completed. The 'is that fair' handshake and the personalized text tieback are the two highest-leverage items she missed. For the text specifically: the customer gave the rep three specific anchors (mother's hospice, electric bill, Marble Falls house) — any one of them in the text transforms it from a calendar reminder into a commitment signal.
